SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper states clear empirical claims (TagLM improves NER and chunking by incorporating bidirectional LM embeddings) and presents experiments on CoNLL datasets with reported F1 gains and LM pretraining details, which supports the main claim. However, critical reproducibility and rigor elements are missing or unclear in the summary: code release is not mentioned, test-set results were withheld during review, and there is no evidence of multiple runs, random seeds, or statistical significance testing to establish robustness of the reported gains. Mathematical content is minimal and appears correct given the method description, but the experimental methodology lacks key controls (e.g., ablations isolating each LM component under controlled seeds) and full computational cost reporting, so conclusions are plausible but not fully substantiated. | B(4): The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments showing sizable F1 gains on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking using bidirectional LM embeddings pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark, and comparisons include strong, recent baselines. There are no nontrivial mathematical derivations to check and the methodology—pretraining forward/backward LMs, concatenating top-layer token embeddings, and adding them to a CRF-based tagger—is coherent and empirically validated. Minor technical concerns remain: the summary does not report significance testing, and the test-set withholding (for anonymity) limits reproducibility until updated; these do not, however, undermine the presented experimental evidence. Overall the experiments and results justify the conclusions, with only small gaps in reported statistical rigor and immediate reproducibility.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper addresses a core NLP/ML venue topic—improving sequence tagging with large-scale semi‑supervised pretraining—and presents clear, broadly relevant contributions (TagLM bidirectional LM embeddings) validated on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) with strong baselines and reported hyperparameters. The evaluation metrics (F1, perplexity), comparison to prior state‑of‑the‑art systems, use of the 1B Word Benchmark for LM pretraining, and discussion of dataset‑specific caveats align well with expectations for a top conference audience. While test‑set withholding for anonymity is noted, the methodological framing, relevance, and empirical claims make it an excellent fit for a major NLP/ML venue. | B(5): This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core sequence-tagging problem (NER and chunking) using large-scale semi-supervised transfer from bidirectional LMs and demonstrates state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003, CoNLL 2000) with appropriate baselines and evaluation metrics. The methodology (pretrained forward/backward LMs, token-level LM embeddings, hierarchical tagger + CRF) and use of the 1B Word Benchmark match community standards, and the paper is clearly targeted to researchers working on representation transfer and sequence labeling. Positioning and related-work coverage are thorough, making it relevant and valuable to the venue's audience.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against a strong set of recent and relevant baselines (Lample et al., Ma & Hovy, Chiu & Nichols, Hashimoto et al., Yang et al.) on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using F1, and reports mean scores showing improvements. The setup appears fair: same labeled data and metrics, and the use of large unlabeled corpora for LM pretraining is appropriate for a semi‑supervised method and is clearly described. The authors acknowledge nuances where prior methods perform comparably (e.g., chunking sensitivity to POS supervision) and analyze differences due to bidirectional LM features. Minor omissions are the absence of code release mention and limited discussion of whether all competing semi‑supervised LM approaches (if any contemporary) were included, so I rate it 4/5 for comparisons. | B(5): The paper compares to a comprehensive set of recent and relevant baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Hashimoto et al. 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016) on the same standard datasets (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using identical evaluation metrics (F1). It quantifies gains from bidirectional LM embeddings (absolute F1 improvements reported) and discusses cases where prior methods perform comparably (e.g., sensitivity in chunking when adding POS supervision), and the related work coverage is described as thorough, indicating fair and well-positioned comparisons.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of recent and relevant baselines (Lample et al., Ma & Hovy, Chiu & Nichols, Hashimoto et al., Yang et al.) on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using F1, and reports mean scores showing improvements. CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized and flows logically from motivation through method (pretraining forward/backward LMs and concatenating top-layer embeddings) to experiments and conclusion; the main contributions are stated early and the methodology is described clearly. Notation and hyperparameter reporting appear consistent and adequate for comprehension, and figures/tables (e.g., results on CoNLL 2003 and CoNLL 2000) are informative. Writing quality is high with few ambiguous or grammatical issues. Minor presentation issues remain (e.g., the note about withholding test-set results for anonymity and the absence of an explicit code release mention slightly reduce reproducibility clarity) | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from motivation to method (TagLM) to experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (augmenting taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings and state-of-the-art results on CoNLL NER/Chunking) are stated early. Notation and methodology are presented consistently (details on LM architectures, concatenation of top-layer embeddings, and CRF output are given) and figures/tables appear effective given the reported metrics and baselines. Writing quality is generally good and related work coverage is thorough, though withholding the test set numbers for anonymity and the noted sensitivity of chunking comparisons introduce minor presentation rough edges. Overall the exposition is clear and accessible to readers in sequence tagging research, with only small clarity issues to address.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable and practical idea—injecting pretrained bidirectional LM token-level embeddings (trained on the 1B Word Benchmark) into a supervised CRF-based sequence tagger—which is a clear extension beyond standard supervised taggers and yields substantial empirical gains on CoNLL NER and chunking. The problem (sequence tagging) is well-studied, but the specific mechanism of separately pretraining forward/backward LMs and concatenating their top-layer outputs as contextual features is a distinct methodological contribution that the authors differentiate from prior work. This is not a radical paradigm shift but is a non-trivial, well-motivated advance that would inform experts working on semi-supervised representation learning for sequence labeling. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and concrete idea—augmenting sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional language-model token embeddings (concatenating separately pretrained forward and backward LM top-layer outputs)—that was not standard in prior NER/chunking pipelines and yields meaningful empirical gains on CoNLL tasks. The problem (sequence tagging) is well-studied, but the method clearly differentiates itself from prior supervised taggers and earlier uses of static or unidirectional LM features by leveraging large-scale bidirectional LM pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark. The approach is a non-trivial combination of existing components (neural LMs + CRF tagger) with novel application and design choices, so it would inform experts though it is not a wholly radical departure from contemporary semi-supervised techniques.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable and practical idea—injecting pretrained bidirectional LM token-level embeddings (trained on the 1B Word Benchmark) into a supervised CRF-based sequence tagger—which is a clear extension beyond standard supervised taggers and yields substantial empirical gains on CoNLL NER and chunking. The problem (sequence tagging) is well-studied, but the specific mechanism of separately pretraining forward/backward LMs and concatenating their top-layer outputs as contextual features is a distinct methodological contribution that the authors differentiate from prior work. This is not a radical paradigm shift but is a non-trivial, well-motivated advance that would inform experts working on semi-supervised representation learning for sequence labeling.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and concrete idea—augmenting sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional language-model token embeddings (concatenating separately pretrained forward and backward LM top-layer outputs)—that was not standard in prior NER/chunking pipelines and yields meaningful empirical gains on CoNLL tasks. The problem (sequence tagging) is well-studied, but the method clearly differentiates itself from prior supervised taggers and earlier uses of static or unidirectional LM features by leveraging large-scale bidirectional LM pretraining on the 1B Word Benchmark. The approach is a non-trivial combination of existing components (neural LMs + CRF tagger) with novel application and design choices, so it would inform experts though it is not a wholly radical departure from contemporary semi-supervised techniques.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important practical problem—improving sequence tagging—by introducing bidirectional LM embeddings (TagLM) and demonstrates substantial, reproducible-seeming gains (≈1.06 F1 on CoNLL 2003 NER and ≈1.37 F1 on CoNLL 2000 Chunking) and new state-of-the-art results using large-scale unlabeled pretraining (1B Word Benchmark). These improvements are likely to change how researchers and practitioners approach feature representations for tagging (augmenting taggers with contextualized LM features) and are broadly applicable to many sequence-labeling tasks. Adoption could be rapid in both research and production, although missing details such as released code and withheld test-set results could slow immediate reproducibility and verification. | B(4): This paper addresses an important problem—improving sequence tagging via semi-supervised transfer—and demonstrates substantial, consistent gains (≈1.06 F1 on CoNLL-2003 NER and 1.37 F1 on CoNLL-2000 chunking) by augmenting taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings trained on 1B Word Benchmark. Showing new state-of-the-art results without additional labeled data or gazetteers suggests practitioners can materially improve taggers by incorporating LM-derived token context features, which will likely be widely adopted and built upon. While not wholly transformative given existing LM transfer work, the clear empirical benefits and practical recipe open useful directions for applying contextualized embeddings to other sequence-labeling tasks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important practical problem—improving sequence tagging—by introducing bidirectional LM embeddings (TagLM) and demonstrates substantial, reproducible-seeming gains (≈1.06 F1 on CoNLL 2003 NER and ≈1.37 F1 on CoNLL 2000 Chunking) and new state-of-the-art results using large-scale unlabeled pretraining (1B Word Benchmark). These improvements are likely to change how researchers and practitioners approach feature representations for tagging (augmenting taggers with contextualized LM features) and are broadly applicable to many sequence-labeling tasks. Adoption could be rapid in both research and production, although missing details such as released code and withheld test-set results could slow immediate reproducibility and verification.

Showing new state-of-the-art results without additional labeled data or gazetteers suggests practitioners can materially improve taggers by incorporating LM-derived token context features, which will likely be widely adopted and built upon. While not wholly transformative given existing LM transfer work, the clear empirical benefits and practical recipe open useful directions for applying contextualized embeddings to other sequence-labeling tasks.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents strong empirical evidence across two standard sequence tagging tasks, uses large-scale unlabeled pretraining (1B Word), reports hyperparameters, and compares to relevant baselines — supporting the main claim that bidirectional LM embeddings improve F1. It also includes some sensitivity comment (POS supervision level for chunking) and reports perplexities for LMs, but important reproducibility and robustness controls are missing: no mention of code release, random seeds or number of runs/statistical significance, limited ablation on LM architecture/size and computational cost, and the test-set withholding harms verifiability. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing but lack a few key reproducibility and sensitivity analyses I would expect. | B(4): The experiments convincingly demonstrate that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings improve sequence tagging, with strong results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking and comparisons to relevant high-quality baselines. The paper reports hyperparameters and LM pretraining details (1B Word Benchmark) and quantifies gains (absolute F1 improvements), and it notes sensitivity of chunking to POS supervision, which is a useful sensitivity comment. However, the summary does not indicate extensive failure analyses or broader ablations (e.g., uni- vs. bi-directional LM component effects or domain transfer tests) and code isn’t mentioned, so reproducibility and exploration of limitations are not exhaustively addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents strong empirical evidence across two standard sequence tagging tasks, uses large-scale unlabeled pretraining (1B Word), reports hyperparameters, and compares to relevant baselines — supporting the main claim that bidirectional LM embeddings improve F1. It also includes some sensitivity comment (POS supervision level for chunking) and reports perplexities for LMs, but important reproducibility and robustness controls are missing: no mention of code release, random seeds or number of runs/statistical significance, limited ablation on LM architecture/size and computational cost, and the test-set withholding harms verifiability. Overall the experiments are solid and convincing but lack a few key reproducibility and sensitivity analyses I would expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments convincingly demonstrate that pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings improve sequence tagging, with strong results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking and comparisons to relevant high-quality baselines. The paper reports hyperparameters and LM pretraining details (1B Word Benchmark) and quantifies gains (absolute F1 improvements), and it notes sensitivity of chunking to POS supervision, which is a useful sensitivity comment. However, the summary does not indicate extensive failure analyses or broader ablations (e.g., uni- vs. bi-directional LM component effects or domain transfer tests) and code isn’t mentioned, so reproducibility and exploration of limitations are not exhaustively addressed.

main_claims: Introducing TagLM: a semi-supervised sequence tagger that augments supervised taggers with pretrained bidirectional language model (LM) embeddings.
TagLM achieves new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (91.93 mean F1) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (96.37 mean F1) without using additional labeled data or task-specific gazetteers.
Adding bidirectional LM embeddings yields substantial average absolute improvements (1.06 F1 for NER and 1.37 F1 for Chunking) over the baseline tagger.

Bidirectional LM embeddings are produced by separately pretraining forward and backward LMs (e.g., CNN-BIG-LSTM forward and LSTM-2048-512 backward) and concatenating their top-layer outputs as token-level context embeddings.
_summary.methodology: Pretrain forward and backward neural language models on large unlabeled corpora (1B Word Benchmark), extract top-layer LM embeddings for each token, concatenate forward and backward embeddings to form bidirectional LM features, and add these as extra inputs to a supervised hierarchical neural sequence tagging model (with a CRF output).
baselines: Baseline hierarchical neural tagging model (bidirectional RNN + CRF)
limitations: Test set performance was withheld to preserve anonymity (to be updated before publication).
Chunking performance is sensitive to the level at which additional POS supervision is added (noted as a factor in related comparisons).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semi-supervised sequence tagging / named entity recognition and chunking

reviewer_a.name: Rigorous Reproducibility Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer and researcher specializing in empirical methodology for NLP; experience building, validating, and deploying sequence tagging systems in both research and production settings.
traits: focuses on experimental rigor: requests clear training/validation/test splits, random seeds, run counts, and statistical significance testing
expects thorough ablation studies and controlled baselines that isolate the effect of each proposed component
values reproducibility: looks for full hyperparameter lists, training curves, and release of code and pretrained models
checks implementation details and computational costs (model sizes, runtime, memory, and training data used) to assess practical feasibility
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Evaluates empirical claims by probing experimental controls, reproducibility, and whether reported gains are robust and attributable to the proposed method.

reviewer_b.name: NLP Sequence Modeling & Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Senior academic researcher focused on sequence labeling, language model transfer, and their applications; tracks advances in semi-supervised methods and how they shift state-of-the-art and practice.
traits: assesses novelty relative to existing LM transfer and semi-supervised tagging approaches and situates work in the literature
values broader scientific and practical impact: scalability to other languages/domains, ease of adoption, and deployment considerations
examines conceptual clarity: whether the paper gives insight into why bidirectional LM embeddings help and limitations of the approach
notices whether experimental comparisons are to the strongest prior work (including recent concurrent models) and whether gains are meaningful beyond incremental tuning
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates novelty, significance, and positioning within the research landscape, emphasizing whether the contribution advances understanding or practice in sequence tagging.
